title: Ensure write level Object Storage logging is enabled for all  buckets

input_row.title: Ensure write level Object Storage logging is enabled for all  buckets
input_row.description: Object Storage write logs will log all write requests made to objects in a bucket.
input_row.rationale: Enabling an Object Storage write log, the requestAction property would contain values of PUT, POST, or DELETE. This will provide you more visibility into changes to objects in your buckets.
input_row.audit: From Console: 1. Log into the OCI console. 2. Select Storage from the Services, and click on Buckets. 3. Click on the individual Bucket under the Name heading. 4. Click Logs from the Resource menu on the left. 5. Click on the slider under Enable Log in row labeled Write Access Events. 6. Select the Compartment. 7. Select the Log Group. 8. Enter a Log Name. 9. Select a Log Retention. 10. Click Enable Log. From CLI: 1. Find the bucket name of the specific bucket. oci os bucket list --compartment-id 2. Find the OCID of the Log Group used for FlowLogs. oci logging log-group list --compartment-id --query "data [?\"display-name\"=='']" 3. List the logs associated with the bucket name for this bucket oci logging log list --log-group-id --query "data [?configuration.source.resource=='']" 4. Ensure a log is listed for this bucket name
input_row.remediation: From Console: First, if a log group for holding these logs has not already been created, create a log group by the following steps: 1. Go to the Log Groups page https://cloud.oracle.com/logging/log-groups 2. Click the Create Log Groups button in the middle of the screen. 3. Select the relevant compartment to place these logs. 4. Type a name for the log group in the Name box. 5. Add an optional description in the Description box. 6. Click the Create button in the lower left-hand corner. Second, enable Object Storage write log logging for your bucket(s) by the following steps: 1. Go to the Logs page https://cloud.oracle.com/logging/logs 2. Click the Enable Service Log button in the middle of the screen. 3. Select the relevant resource compartment. 4. Select Object Storage from the Service drop down menu. 5. Select the relevant bucket from the resource drop down menu. 6. Select 'Write Access Events` from the Log Category drop down menu. 7. Type a name for your Object Storage write log in the Log Name drop down menu. 8. Click the Enable Log button in the lower left-hand corner. From CLI: First, if a log group for holding these logs has not already been created, create a log group by the following steps: 1. Create a log group: oci logging log-group create --compartment-id --display-name "" --description "" The output of the command gives you a work request id. You can query the work request to see the status of the job by issuing the following command: oci logging work-request get --work-request-id Look for status filed to be SUCCEEDED. Second, enable Object Storage write log logging for your bucket(s) by the following steps: 2. Get the Log group ID needed for creating the Log: oci logging log-group list --compartment-id --query 'data[?contains("display-name", `'""'`)].id|join(`\n`, @)' -- raw-output 3. Create a JSON file called config.json with the following content: { "compartment-id":"", "source": { "resource": " --display-name "" --log-type SERVICE --is-enabled TRUE --configuration file://config.json The output of the command gives you a work request id. You can query the work request to see that status of the job by issuing the following command: oci logging work-request get --work-request-id Look for the status filed to be SUCCEEDED.
input_row.impact: There is no performance impact when enabling the above described features, but will generate additional use of object storage that should be controlled via object lifecycle management. By default, Object Storage logs are stored for 30 days in object storage. Users can specify a longer retention period.
input_row.justification: The second AI opinion correctly identifies that the original control is intended for Oracle Cloud Infrastructure, but the task is to apply it to AWS. AWS provides the necessary APIs through `boto3` to automate the verification of S3 bucket logging. Specifically, AWS S3 bucket logging can be checked using `boto3` methods to ensure compliance with the control by verifying if write operations are logged. This makes the process automatable using AWS services.
input_row.automation_summary: - **AWS Resource/Service:** S3 Buckets - **API/CLI Method:** `boto3 s3.get_bucket_logging(Bucket='bucket-name')` - **Properties to Check:** `LoggingEnabled` field in the response - **Pass Criteria:** `LoggingEnabled` field is present and correctly configured - **Fail Criteria:** `LoggingEnabled` field is absent or incorrectly configured, indicating that write-level logging is not enabled

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** oci_object_storage_bucket_write_logging_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**

- **OCI API/SDK/CLI Usage:**
  - Enumerate all Object Storage buckets in all relevant compartments using the OCI CLI (`oci os bucket list --compartment-id <compartment_ocid>`) or Python SDK (`list_buckets()`).
  - For each bucket, list all logs in compartments and log groups (`oci logging log list --log-group-id <log_group_ocid>`) and filter for logs where:
    - `configuration.source.resource` matches the bucket’s name or OCID.
    - `log-type` is `SERVICE`.
    - Log category (if available) or configuration parameters indicate "Write Access Events" (i.e., the log captures write operations: PUT, POST, DELETE).
    - Log must be enabled (`is-enabled` == true).
  - Confirm each bucket has at least one enabled log for write access events.
- **Pass Criteria:**
  - Every Object Storage bucket must have an enabled log that records write-level operations (PUT, POST, DELETE, etc.) in an OCI Log Group.
- **Fail Criteria:**
  - Any bucket missing an enabled write-level log, or with a log disabled, is out of compliance.
- **Additional Notes:**
  - If using the Python SDK: Use `oci.logging.LoggingManagementClient` to list log groups and logs, and inspect the log configuration for object storage write events.
  - Retention period and log group settings can be validated per policy but are not strictly required for this check unless specified.
  - Cross-check findings against audit logs if additional evidence required.
- **Example CLI Audit Flow:**
  1. List buckets:  
     `oci os bucket list --compartment-id <compartment_ocid>`
  2. List log groups:  
     `oci logging log-group list --compartment-id <compartment_ocid>`
  3. List logs per log group:  
     `oci logging log list --log-group-id <log_group_ocid>`
  4. Inspect log configuration for each log for association with the bucket and verify `is-enabled` and that it logs "Write Access Events".

**SOURCE:** CIS_Oracle_Cloud_Infrastructure_Foundations_Benchmark_v3_0_0
step2_review:
It appears there is a misunderstanding in the context of your request. The audit assessment provided is related to Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI), not AWS. Therefore, references to AWS services, boto3, and AWS CLI are not applicable in this context. I will proceed with the review based on OCI capabilities.

**1. APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE
   - The classification as "Automated" is correct. The use of OCI CLI and Python SDK for checking the logging configuration of Object Storage buckets aligns with an automated approach.
   - This cannot be checked via AWS APIs/boto3/CLI as this is specific to OCI.

**2. TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8
   - The automation details are mostly technically correct. The use of OCI CLI commands and Python SDK methods for listing buckets and logs is appropriate.
   - The program name `oci_object_storage_bucket_write_logging_enabled` is descriptive and follows a clear naming convention, although it could be more specific by including the term "write_access_events".
   - The steps for verifying the log configuration are accurate, but the explanation could be more detailed regarding how to filter logs specifically for write operations.

**3. IMPROVEMENTS:**
   - The program name could be improved to `oci_object_storage_bucket_write_access_logging_enabled` to explicitly indicate the focus on write access events.
   - The assessment could benefit from a more detailed explanation of how to filter and identify logs that specifically capture write operations (PUT, POST, DELETE).
   - Consider adding a step to verify the retention period of logs if it is a compliance requirement.
   - Ensure that the audit flow includes error handling and logging of the audit process itself for traceability.

**4. CONFIDENCE:** HIGH
   - The assessment is well-structured and uses appropriate OCI services and methods. The automation approach is sound and aligns with OCI's capabilities.

**5. FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**
   - Modify: Update the program name to `oci_object_storage_bucket_write_access_logging_enabled` for clarity.
   - Add more detailed instructions on filtering logs for write operations.
   - Consider including a verification step for log retention settings if applicable to the compliance requirements.

Overall, the assessment is technically sound and effectively utilizes OCI's capabilities for an automated compliance check. The suggested improvements are minor and aimed at enhancing clarity and completeness.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**
The compliance control for ensuring write-level Object Storage logging is enabled can be effectively automated using the OCI CLI or Python SDK, as both AI opinions confirm the feasibility of this approach. The detailed steps provided in the first opinion align with the capabilities of OCI APIs, allowing for efficient verification of logging configurations across multiple buckets.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
oracle_object_storage_bucket_write_access_logging_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- OCI Resource/Service: Object Storage Buckets
- API/CLI Method: `oci os bucket list` for listing buckets and `oci logging log list` for checking log configurations
- Properties to Check: 
  - `is-enabled` should be true for write access logs
  - Log type should be `SERVICE`
  - Log category should indicate "Write Access Events" (PUT, POST, DELETE)
- Pass Criteria: Every Object Storage bucket must have at least one enabled log that records write-level operations.
- Fail Criteria: Any bucket missing an enabled write-level log, or with a log disabled, is out of compliance.
